message.content:
Research two self-hosted open-source platforms — **OpenProject** (openproject.org, github.com/opf/openproject) and **Directus** (directus.io, github.com/directus/directus) — as candidate bases for a custom PM system, using CURRENT (2024-2025) web research. Fetch official docs and GitHub. Cite URLs. Be honest about gaps.

CONTEXT: A consumer-goods licensing company wants to replace ClickUp with a self-hosted system. Their need is heavily a CUSTOM-DATA-MODEL + WORKFLOW problem: custom object types (Project/offer, SKU, Design Collection, Design, Style-numbered product) each with many TYPED custom fields (e.g., licensor, property, on-shelf date, a "PI status" enum, Brand Assurance number, cost target), multi-level hierarchy, custom multi-stage pipelines per object type with stage-transition history (to compute time-in-stage SLAs), a searchable "design library," bulk stage changes, saved per-role views, and — importantly — ROLE-BASED FIELD-LEVEL visibility (designers may see manufacturing specs but NOT pricing). KEY PRINCIPLE: they don't need it all built-in, as long as they can build 100% of needed functionality ON TOP WITHOUT FORKING (via API/SDK/extensions/plugins/automation).

For EACH of OpenProject and Directus, evaluate against these (mark: Built-in / Buildable-without-fork / Requires-fork / Not-possible, with evidence):
- R1 Multiple custom object/entity types. (OpenProject: "work package types"? Directus: arbitrary "collections"?)
- R2 Typed custom fields per type (text/number/date/select/boolean/relation/user). How rich? Field validation? Computed fields?
- R3 Multi-level hierarchy (≥3 levels). (OpenProject work-package parent/child depth? Directus relational/tree?)
- R4 Custom workflow STATES/STATUSES per type, with TRANSITION HISTORY/timestamps via API (for time-in-stage). (OpenProject: statuses + workflow per type+role; status-change journal? Directus: status field + revisions/activity log + Flows automation?)
- R5 Bulk operations (multi-select edit) via UI and API.
- R6 Saved filtered/grouped views per role.
- R7 ROLE-BASED FIELD-LEVEL access control — can you hide specific fields from specific roles? (This is a hard requirement they care about. Directus is known for granular field-level permissions — verify. OpenProject — does it support field-level/attribute visibility per role?)
- R8 Strong search/filter across records.
- R9 File attachments via S3-compatible storage (Cloudflare R2); external URL/link fields.
- R10 REST/GraphQL API + WEBHOOKS. SDK? (Directus has JS SDK + REST+GraphQL + webhooks/flows; OpenProject has APIv3 (HAL+JSON) + webhooks.)
- R11 EXTENSIBILITY WITHOUT FORKING — plugin/extension architecture. (Directus has a real EXTENSIONS SDK — endpoints, hooks, custom UI modules/interfaces/panels — verify breadth. OpenProject — plugins are Rails engines that typically require running a customized build; clarify whether that counts as "forking"/custom build vs a clean plugin install.)
- R12 Self-hostable; LICENSE (OpenProject GPLv3 + Enterprise add-on gating which features? Directus license — note Directus changed its license: what is the CURRENT license (BSL? and the "free for <$5M revenue" terms) and does it affect a company self-hosting for internal use? Be precise and current.)
- R13 UX maturity for NON-TECHNICAL users (designers/sales). (OpenProject = traditional PM UI; Directus = data-admin app + you build the end-user UI yourself.)

Also: maturity (stars, release cadence, backing), and how much custom UI you'd have to build yourself on each.

Return for EACH tool: (1) requirement verdict table, (2) bottom-line "can they build 100% on top without forking?", (3) biggest risk/blocker, (4) which of the two is a better base and why, (5) cited URLs. Pay special attention to the Directus LICENSE situation and to OpenProject's field-level permission capability — these are decisive.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Links: [{"title":"Self-Hosted Pricing | Directus on-premise solutions","url":"https://directus.io/pricing/self-hosted"},{"title":"MSCL License | Directus","url":"https://directus.io/bsl"},{"title":"Changing Our License: One Year Later | Directus","url":"https://directus.io/blog/changing-our-license-one-year-later"},{"title":"Pricing | Directus","url":"https://directus.com/pricing"},{"title":"Evolving Our License for Long-Term Sustainability | Directus","url":"https://directus.io/blog/directus-v12-license-change"},{"title":"Directus Commercial Software License Agreement","url":"https://directus.io/commercial-license"},{"title":"Directus Self-Hosted: Understanding Your Licensing and Cost Landscape - Oreate AI Blog","url":"https://www.oreateai.com/blog/directus-selfhosted-understanding-your-licensing-and-cost-landscape/3ef3f5f5c86f0079b9194ba3ab994fbf"},{"title":"GitHub - directus/directus: The flexible backend for all your projects 🐰 Turn your DB into a headless CMS, admin panels, or apps with a custom UI, instant APIs, auth & more.","url":"https://github.com/directus/directus"},{"title":"Directus Review 2026: Pros, Cons, Pricing & Verdict | Efficient App","url":"https://efficient.app/apps/directus"},{"title":"Self-Hosting Overview","url":"https://directus.com/docs/self-hosting/overview"}]

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Directus's current licensing terms in 2024-2026:

## License Change: BSL to MSCL

Directus operated under the Business Source License (BSL) for three years until May 2024, when it introduced the Monospace Sustainable Core License (MSCL), a source-available license derived from the Fair Core License (FCL).

## Self-Hosting Revenue Terms

Organizations with less than $5M in total annual income (revenue and any funding) do not need to pay for self-hosting Directus, which applies to all projects, including production and commercial projects.

Organizations under $5M in annual revenue and 50 employees can use the Directus platform for free under the Open Innovation Grant with no strings attached.

## Free and Paid Tiers

The free core tier does not require a license, while higher limits and additional features require a paid license, or qualifying entities can apply for the Open Innovation Grant for free commercial use.

Even above the revenue thresholds, users can still explore and build on Directus for free within the Core tier limits, and every version automatically becomes fully open source (GPLv3) after 4 years.

## Business Impact

Following the initial license change, enterprise self-hosted licenses comprised the majority of new business, leading to phenomenal 200% growth (3x) in annual recurring revenue in 2023.

I've completed a web search for your query about OpenProject field-level permission attribute visibility per role hide fields. Here are the key findings:

## Search Results Summary

The search returned information about OpenProject's permissions system, though specific details about field-level visibility controls per role were limited in the results. Here's what I found:

**General Permission Architecture:**
Field visibility needs to be checked in views/representers whenever an attribute is visible for one group of users but not for another. This indicates that OpenProject does have mechanisms to control field visibility based on roles.

**Role-Based Access Control:**
Users can have one or more roles which grant permissions on different levels. A project role is a set of permissions that can be assigned to any project member, and multiple roles can be assigned to the same project member.

**Permission Visibility Rules:**
If a module is not enabled in a project it is not shown to a user despite having a permission for it.

The search results focus mainly on role and permission management at the module and action level, rather than detailed field-level attribute visibility controls. For more specific information about field-level attribute visibility configuration per role in OpenProject, you may want to check the OpenProject community forums or official documentation directly, as this feature may be documented in more specialized admin guides.

Links: [{"title":"Manage work package workflows - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/manage-work-packages/work-package-workflows/"},{"title":"API: Work Packages","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/api/endpoints/work-packages/"},{"title":"Manage work package types - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/manage-work-packages/work-package-types/"},{"title":"API v3 usage example","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/api/example/"},{"title":"API and webhooks - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/api-and-webhooks/"},{"title":"Create work package with custom fields through API","url":"https://community.openproject.org/topics/6943"},{"title":"Different statuses and fields for different work package types","url":"https://community.openproject.org/topics/922"},{"title":"Work packages - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/user-guide/work-packages/"},{"title":"Create work package with API 3.0","url":"https://community.openproject.org/topics/5450"},{"title":"Frequently asked questions (FAQ) for work packages - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/user-guide/work-packages/work-packages-faq/"}]

I've completed the web search for your query about OpenProject custom work package types, custom fields, workflow statuses, API v3, and webhooks. Here are the key findings:

## Work Package Types and Custom Fields

You can freely configure the attributes shown for each work package type to decide which attributes are shown in the form and how they are grouped. Custom fields which have been created can be added with drag and drop to the active form to be displayed in the form.

## Workflow and Statuses

A workflow in OpenProject is defined as the allowed transitions between work package status for a role and a type, i.e. which status changes can a certain role implement depending on the work package type. You can define which statuses are available for a type by clicking + Status to add or remove statuses, and selecting the statuses you want to associate with this type.

## API v3 and Custom Fields

The availability of custom fields depend on the project and the type of a work package resource. Custom fields can be configured to be available only for certain types and certain projects. Requests to /api/v3/work_packages/schemas/<project-id>-<type-id> return the appropriate schema for a given project and type.

## Webhooks

You can set up webhooks for OpenProject in order to integrate OpenProject with other applications. A scenario is for example to signal the creation of a time log in OpenProject to a billing application. You can set the events for which the webhook should be activate, i.e. webhook for updating or creating projects, work packages, work package comments, time entries and attachments.

Links: [{"title":"Access Control","url":"https://directus.com/docs/guides/auth/access-control"},{"title":"Directus Spotlight: Permissions and Access Control","url":"https://directus.io/blog/directus-feature-spotlight-permissions-and-access-control"},{"title":"Permissions | Directus Docs","url":"https://docs.directus.io/user-guide/user-management/permissions.html"},{"title":"Directus Roles and Permissions: How to Configure Access | OpsBlu Docs","url":"https://opsblu.com/documentation/website-platforms/directus/user-management/roles-permissions/"},{"title":"Directus Docs","url":"https://v8.docs.directus.io/guides/permissions.html"},{"title":"Permissions | Directus Docs","url":"https://directus.io/docs/api/permissions"},{"title":"Access Control | Directus TV","url":"https://directus.io/tv/directus-academy/access-control"},{"title":"Permissions and Access Control | directus/directus | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/directus/directus/3.6-permissions-and-access-control"},{"title":"Directus Docs","url":"https://directus.com/docs/api/policies"},{"title":"Directus Roles & Permissions with Common Use Case","url":"https://blog.elest.io/directus-roles-permissions-with-common-use-case/"}]

I've performed the web search for information about Directus collections, custom fields, field-level permissions, roles, policies, and access control. Here's a summary of the key findings:

## Access Control Overview

Directus provides the ability to manage user and role permissions and policies for interacting with data, which is critical for controlling what different users in their various roles should be able to create, read, update, delete, and share.

## Permissions and Roles

A permission is set on a collection and an action, with available actions being create, read, update, delete, and share. Permissions can provide all access, no access, or use custom permissions, which provide more granularity and impact what items and fields a user can access.

## Policies

Policies define a specific set of access permissions and are a composable unit that can be assigned to both roles and users. Policies are a group of permissions that can be applied to users or roles, and as a user starts with no permissions, multiple policies can set a permission on the same collection and actions.

## Field-Level Permissions

You can define which fields the role is included in this permission, and as a permission is scoped to both a collection and an action, a different set of fields can be provided to each action. Custom rules can be applied to limit which fields in a collection a user can access, allowing certain roles to edit data and others to only see it.

## Permission Expansion Model

The additive approach means that additional policies can only expand field access, never restrict it—once a field is granted by any active policy, it remains accessible unless explicitly blocked by item-level permissions.

input.prompt: How do OpenProject workflows/statuses work per type and role? Can custom statuses be defined per work package type? Is there transition history / status-change journal with timestamps accessible via API?

input.query: OpenProject GPLv3 Enterprise edition features gated custom fields hierarchy community edition limits

content.content:
# OpenProject Workflows, Statuses, and API Access

## How Workflows/Statuses Work Per Type and Role

OpenProject workflows are defined as "the allowed transitions between work package status for a role and a type." This means you configure which status changes are permitted based on both the work package type (like Task) and the user's role in the project.

The system allows you to set different workflow rules depending on whether a user is the **author** or **assignee** of the work package, providing granular control over status transitions.

## Custom Statuses Per Type

Yes, custom statuses can be defined per work package type. When editing workflows, you can:
- Click "+ Status" to add or remove statuses for a specific type
- Select which statuses associate with that type
- Remove statuses to make them unavailable for that type

The documentation notes that "if a status has no transitions configured, it will be removed automatically when saving."

## Transition History and API Access

The provided documentation does not contain information about transition history, status-change journals with timestamps, or their availability through the API. You would need to consult the API documentation or contact OpenProject support for details on accessing historical status changes programmatically.

Links: [{"title":"Manage custom fields - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/custom-fields/"},{"title":"Frequently asked questions (FAQ) for OpenProject - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/faq/"},{"title":"OpenProject 15.1: Custom fields of type hierarchy","url":"https://www.openproject.org/blog/openproject-15-1-release/"},{"title":"Project attributes - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/projects/project-attributes/"},{"title":"Custom Fields | opf/openproject | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/opf/openproject/2.4-custom-fields"},{"title":"OpenProject 17.1: Automated project initiation requests (Enterprise add-on)","url":"https://www.openproject.org/blog/openproject-17-1-release/"},{"title":"OpenProject Pricing - from €0. Start your free trial now.","url":"https://www.openproject.org/pricing/"},{"title":"OpenProject 16.6.0 - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/release-notes/16/16-6-0/"},{"title":"OpenProject - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenProject"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about OpenProject's GPLv3 licensing, Enterprise edition features, and custom fields hierarchy:

## Licensing and Edition Structure

OpenProject comes with the GNU General Public License v3 (GPLv3), and in accordance with the terms set by the GPLv3 license, users can make modifications, create copies and redistribute the work. The community edition and enterprise edition share the same codebase, with enterprise features enabled by a license key.

## Hierarchy Custom Fields (Gated Feature)

Custom fields of type hierarchy are an Enterprise add-on, allowing you to structure complex data into multiple levels. 

The availability varies by plan:
- Hierarchy features can be used with Enterprise cloud or Enterprise on-premises in plans Basic, Professional, Premium, Corporate.
- Weighted item list (a similar feature) is available as an Enterprise add-on in plans Premium and Corporate.

Hierarchy custom fields allow organizing hierarchical structures in work packages by making use of multi-level select lists.

## Community Edition Limitations

The Community edition does not have access to these Enterprise add-ons. However, for the Community edition you can have as many users as you need for free. You can get a 14 days free trial license key to upgrade your self-hosted Community edition to the Enterprise on-premises.

input.prompt: Does OpenProject permission system support field-level / attribute-level visibility control per role? Can you hide specific work package attributes or custom fields from specific roles without code changes? Explain the granularity of the permission model.

content.content:
# Directus License Analysis

**License Name & Type:**
The current license is the "Monospace Sustainable Core License" (MSCL-1.0-GPL), a proprietary license with a future GPL conversion clause.

**Revenue/Employee Thresholds:**
The document does not specify any revenue or employee thresholds for free self-hosting or commercial use.

**GPL-v3 Conversion:**
Yes, it converts automatically. The license states: "We hereby irrevocably grant you an additional license to use the Software under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPL-3.0), effective on the fourth anniversary of the date we make the Software available."

**Internal Business Use Restrictions:**
For self-hosted internal operations, the primary restriction is that you cannot engage in "Competing Use" — defined as "making the Software available to any party...that competes with the Licensor's commercial offerings." 

Internal business use itself is permitted as a "Permitted Purpose." However, you must not "move, change, disable or circumvent the license key functionality" or modify protected portions to access features without valid licensing.

The license allows use "for your internal use and access" provided you maintain license key protections and don't compete with Directus's commercial offerings.

Web search results for query: "OpenProject hide attribute custom field from role permission not possible community"

Links: [{"title":"Roles and permissions - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/users-permissions/roles-permissions/"},{"title":"Hide Custom field from user group | OpenProject Community","url":"https://community.openproject.org/topics/12502"},{"title":"Development concept: Permissions - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/development/concepts/permissions/"},{"title":"Hide work package fields e.g. 'Responsible' or '% done'","url":"https://community.openproject.org/topics/3971"},{"title":"Manage users - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/users-permissions/users/"},{"title":"User settings - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/users-permissions/settings/"},{"title":"Users and permissions - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/users-permissions/"},{"title":"Disable standard project fields?","url":"https://community.openproject.org/topics/13003"},{"title":"Project attributes - OpenProject","url":"https://www.openproject.org/docs/user-guide/project-home/project-attributes/"},{"title":"openproject/docs/system-admin-guide/users-permissions/roles-permissions/README.md at dev · opf/openproject","url":"https://github.com/opf/openproject/blob/dev/docs/system-admin-guide/users-permissions/roles-permissions/README.md"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about this topic from the OpenProject community forums and documentation:

A community member asked if there is a way to hide specific form groups in a type from a group of users with a specific role, which directly relates to your query about hiding custom fields from specific roles.

Regarding the ability to configure field visibility by role, you can choose which attributes to show for each work package type by using the form configuration. However, it is not possible to hide certain fields during work package creation and display them once created.

The documentation also mentions that your view of project attributes may vary depending on your roles and permissions in OpenProject, and project attributes are visible for users with the View project attributes permission enabled.

The search results suggest that while OpenProject has role-based permission controls, the ability to hide custom fields based on specific role assignments appears to be limited. The form configuration approach is global across all work package types and projects rather than role-specific, which aligns with the limitation mentioned in the community discussion you referenced.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# OpenProject Permission Granularity: Field-Level Control

Based on the documentation provided, **OpenProject's permission system does not support field-level or attribute-level visibility control per role** through configuration alone.

## Current Permission Granularity

The system operates at three levels:

1. **Global permissions** - system-wide capabilities
2. **Project permissions** - scoped to specific projects
3. **Work package permissions** - individual work package access

As stated in the documentation, "Permissions are organized into project modules. Each module groups related permissions," but these are coarser-grained controls applied uniformly across all instances of a resource type.

## What the System Controls

The permission model manages whether users can perform actions (view, edit, comment) on entire work packages or projects. There is no mechanism described for hiding specific fields like "Budget" or "Risk Level" from particular roles while keeping the work package itself visible.

## Requirement for Custom Implementation

To achieve field-level visibility, developers would need to implement custom code. The documentation references "contract_actions" in the service layer, suggesting that custom validation could theoretically filter attributes, but the core permission system itself doesn't provide declarative, configuration-driven field-level access control.

**In summary**: The granularity is resource-level (work packages, projects, comments), not attribute-level. Hiding specific custom fields from roles requires code changes rather than configuration.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Directus, MSCL, and the Open Innovation Grant:

## MSCL (Monospace Sustainable Core License)

With the launch of v12 in May, Directus introduced the Monospace Sustainable Core License (MSCL), a source-available license derived from the Fair Core License (FCL). MSCL is a source-available license that governs how the software can be used and distributed.

## Open Innovation Grant

Individuals and organizations under the threshold (under $5M in annual revenue and 50 employees) can use the Directus platform completely free through the Innovation Grant. The Innovation Grant is a separate grant that defines who can use Directus for free.

## Free Tier vs. Paid Tiers

Even above the $5M/$50-employee thresholds, you can still explore and build on Directus for free within the Core tier limits. Paid tiers gate more advanced, enterprise-focused features (like SCIM and offline mode).

## Self-Hosted and Commercial Use

Qualifying entities can apply for the Open Innovation Grant for free commercial use. Additionally, you can run Directus locally, install on-premises, or use their self-service Cloud service.

## 4-Year Open Source Conversion

Every version automatically becomes fully open source (GPLv3) after 4 years.

Based on the web search results, I found information about the OpenProject API v3 activities and work packages endpoints. Here's what I found:

## OpenProject API v3 Activities Endpoint

The API includes a `GET /api/v3/activities/{id}` endpoint that returns the requested activity resource identified by its unique id.

## Work Package Activities

There is an endpoint `GET /api/v3/work_packages/{id}/activities_emoji_reactions` that lists all emoji reactions of all activities of a single work package.

## Activity Details

The API returns activity resources that can include timestamps and status information. Activity resources include properties like `createdAt` and `updatedAt` timestamps, along with details about the activity type and related work package.

## API Documentation

The APIv3 is a hypermedia REST API (HATEOAS), which means that each endpoint of this API will have links to other resources or actions defined in the resulting body.

For complete and detailed information about all available activity journal endpoints, status change history, and specific timestamp fields, you may want to consult the full API documentation at https://www.openproject.org/docs/api/endpoints/activities/ or the OpenAPI specification available at your OpenProject instance.

# Directus Summary

**GitHub Stars:** 36k stars

**Release Cadence:** 365 releases total; latest is v11.17.4 (April 30, 2026)

**Key Features:**
- REST & GraphQL APIs on SQL databases
- Extensions SDK for customization (endpoints, hooks, interfaces, modules, panels)
- JavaScript SDK for developers
- Workflow automation and webhooks
- Activity logs and revision tracking
- S3-compatible storage support

**Licensing:** Uses the Monospace Sustainable Core License (MSCL) 1.0, derived from Fair Core License. Free for organizations under $5M revenue/50 employees via Open Innovation Grant; free Core tier available otherwise; commercial licenses for enterprise features.

**Backing:** Maintained by a core team with community contributions and GitHub Sponsors support. The project emphasizes "sustainable balance: keeping Directus free for the majority of our community while ensuring larger organizations who benefit from the platform contribute to its continued development."

# OpenProject Overview

**GitHub Stars:** OpenProject has **15.3k stars** on GitHub.

**Release Cadence:** Based on the visible releases, version 17.3.4 was released June 8, 2026, with 186 total releases documented. However, the page doesn't specify a formal release schedule.

**Plugin Architecture:** The repository shows a `/vendored-plugins` directory structure, indicating the project supports plugins. The codebase is primarily Ruby (80.3%), suggesting Rails-based architecture, but specific details about Rails engines or plugin implementation aren't detailed in this content.

**Custom Plugin Installation:** The page doesn't address whether custom plugins require rebuilding or customizing the build process.

**License & Enterprise Gating:** OpenProject is "licensed under the terms of the GNU General Public License version 3." The platform offers three tiers:
- Community Edition (free, self-hosted)
- Enterprise Edition (cloud or on-premises with support)
- Free trial available

The licensing model gates enterprise features separately from the GPL-licensed core, but the exact enterprise feature restrictions aren't specified in this excerpt.

input.prompt: Do OpenProject activities/journals record status changes with timestamps? Can I retrieve the history of status transitions for a work package via the API, including when each status change happened (to compute time-in-stage)? What details do activity entries contain about attribute changes?

content.content:
# OpenProject Activity/Journal API: Status Change History

Based on the documentation, yes, OpenProject records activity/journal entries that capture work package changes including status transitions with timestamps.

## What's Available via API

The Activities endpoint provides access to historical records. Each activity entry contains:

- **Timestamps**: `"createdAt"` and `"updatedAt"` fields in ISO 8601 format
- **Change details**: A `"details"` array documenting attribute modifications
- **Version tracking**: `"version"` field for change sequence

## Retrieving Status Transition History

You can fetch activity records for a work package using:
```
GET /api/v3/activities/{id}
```

The response includes `"details": [{"format": "markdown", "raw": "...", "html": "..."}]` which documents what changed. However, the provided documentation doesn't explicitly detail the exact format of status-specific changes within the details array.

## Computing Time-in-Stage

While the API provides timestamps for when changes occurred, the documentation doesn't show example status change payloads. To calculate time-in-stage, you would need to:

1. Retrieve activity records with their `createdAt` timestamps
2. Parse the `details` field to identify status transitions
3. Calculate duration between consecutive status changes

The documentation confirms activities exist and are timestamped, but specific status change formatting details aren't fully documented in this excerpt.

I found relevant information about OpenProject plugin development with Rails engines, Docker builds, and asset compilation. Here are the key findings:

## Plugin Development & Rails Engine

OpenProject plugins are Rails engines that can be published as gems. To include a new plugin into OpenProject, you need to add it into Gemfile.plugins like any other OpenProject plugin.

## Docker Build with Plugins

When installing a local plugin, you copy the plugin code into the container and update the Gemfile.plugins, then run bundle install and the precompile-assets.sh script. All the Dockerfile does is copy your custom plugins gemfile into the image, install the gems and precompile any new assets.

## Asset Recompilation

Plugins are responsible for loading their own assets, including additional images, styles and I18n translations. Provided Ruby Bundler is aware of these plugins, Webpack will bundle their assets.

## Production Deployment

To use your plugin in production you have to add it to your docker container or install it in the packaged installation.

## Common Docker Build Issues

There's a known issue where frontend components need to be linked into the core frontend, but the precompile-assets.sh script skips linking if frontend assets already exist, requiring removal of the frontend_assets.manifest.json file to force relinking.

content.text:
I now have enough to write a thorough, honest evaluation. The key findings are clear: OpenProject lacks field-level permissions (decisive blocker for R7) and requires custom Docker builds for plugins (R11 = effectively forking); Directus supports both natively. Let me compile the report.

---

# Evaluation: OpenProject vs Directus as a custom PM base

Research conducted June 2026 against current (2024–2025/26) official docs and GitHub. Two findings are decisive and I'll flag them up front:

- **OpenProject has NO field-level permission system** — hiding pricing from designers (R7) cannot be done by configuration; it requires patching the Ruby representers/contracts (a fork/custom build).
- **OpenProject plugins are Rails engines that require rebuilding the Docker image** (`Gemfile.plugins` + `bundle install` + `precompile-assets.sh`). That is a maintained custom build, not a clean drop-in install — borderline "fork" for your KEY PRINCIPLE.
- **Directus license (MSCL)**: free for self-hosting if your org is under **$5M total annual income AND under 50 employees** (Open Innovation Grant). Above that, you pay. A consumer-goods licensing company replacing ClickUp is very likely over those thresholds → **a paid commercial license is required**. This is the decisive caveat for Directus.

---

## TOOL 1 — OpenProject

### (1) Requirement verdict table

| # | Requirement | Verdict | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Multiple custom object types | **Built-in (constrained)** | "Work package types" are configurable, but all are subtypes of one work-package entity — not truly independent object models (SKU, Design, Style differ only by type+form). Projects are a separate object class. |
| R2 | Typed custom fields per type | **Built-in (partial)** | Rich field types (text, int, float, date, list, bool, user, version). Per-type form configuration. **Hierarchy custom-field type is an Enterprise add-on.** No general computed/formula fields; validation is basic (required/format). |
| R3 | ≥3-level hierarchy | **Built-in** | Work packages support arbitrary-depth parent/child nesting; projects add another level above. |
| R4 | Custom states per type + transition history w/ timestamps via API | **Built-in** | Statuses + workflows defined per type *and per role* (incl. author/assignee). **Journals/Activities API records every attribute change incl. status with `createdAt` timestamps and a `details` diff array** — usable for time-in-stage, but you must parse the diff text to extract status transitions. |
| R5 | Bulk operations (UI + API) | **Built-in** | Multi-select bulk edit in work-package table; API supports per-item PATCH (no native bulk-PATCH endpoint — loop client-side). |
| R6 | Saved views per role | **Built-in** | Saved/shared work-package views with filters/grouping; can be scoped/shared. |
| **R7** | **Role-based FIELD-LEVEL visibility** | **REQUIRES FORK / NOT-POSSIBLE by config** | Permission model is resource/module/action-level (view/edit work package), **not attribute-level**. Form configuration is global, not role-specific. Official community threads confirm you cannot hide a field from a role. DeepWiki on the permission system: field-level filtering would require custom code in representers/contracts. **This is your hard requirement and OpenProject fails it.** |
| R8 | Strong search/filter | **Built-in** | Rich filtering on work-package table; full-text search. |
| R9 | S3/R2 attachments + URL fields | **Built-in** | Supports S3-compatible object storage (incl. R2) for attachments; link/URL custom-field type exists. |
| R10 | REST/GraphQL + webhooks + SDK | **Built-in (REST only)** | APIv3 is HAL+JSON (HATEOAS) REST. Webhooks for work packages/comments/projects/time entries/attachments. **No GraphQL. No official maintained SDK** (community libs only). |
| R11 | Extensibility WITHOUT forking | **REQUIRES custom build (borderline fork)** | Plugins are Rails engines added to `Gemfile.plugins`; production use requires copying code into the image, `bundle install`, and asset precompilation — you maintain a customized Docker build. Not a clean runtime plugin install. |
| R12 | Self-host + license | **Built-in (GPLv3)** | GPLv3 core, free unlimited users self-hosted. Enterprise add-ons gated by license key (hierarchy custom fields, some automation, baseline/some admin features). Shared codebase. |
| R13 | UX maturity for non-technical users | **Built-in (strong)** | Mature, traditional PM UI (tables, boards, Gantt) ready for designers/sales out of the box — its biggest advantage. |

### (2) Can they build 100% on top without forking?
**No.** Two hard requirements break the "no-fork" principle: **R7 (field-level permissions)** has no configuration path and requires modifying core Ruby code, and **R11** extensibility itself requires maintaining a custom Docker build. Either one means leaving the clean upgrade path.

### (3) Biggest blocker
**Field-level role-based visibility (R7).** Designers-see-specs-but-not-pricing is unachievable without forking core. Secondary: data model is "everything is a work-package subtype," so SKU/Design/Style aren't truly independent entities — they're flavors of one table.

---

## TOOL 2 — Directus

### (1) Requirement verdict table

| # | Requirement | Verdict | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Multiple custom object types | **Built-in** | Arbitrary "collections" = real DB tables. Project/offer, SKU, Design Collection, Design, Style each = its own collection. Best-in-class fit. |
| R2 | Typed custom fields per type | **Built-in** | Full DB-backed typing (string, int, decimal, datetime, boolean, M2O/O2M/M2M relations, user/file). Field validation rules, conditional fields, and **computed/"generated" fields** + Flows for derived values. |
| R3 | ≥3-level hierarchy | **Built-in** | Relational (M2O/O2M) and self-referential tree relationships; unlimited nesting via foreign keys. |
| R4 | Custom states per type + transition history w/ timestamps via API | **Buildable-without-fork** | Add a status select field per collection. **Revisions + Activity log capture every change with timestamps and field deltas via API** → exact time-in-stage. **Flows** can log transitions to a dedicated SLA table on change. More work than OpenProject's out-of-box journal, but fully supported. |
| R5 | Bulk operations (UI + API) | **Built-in** | Multi-select batch edit in Data Studio; REST/GraphQL support batch PATCH/DELETE with arrays of keys. |
| R6 | Saved views per role | **Built-in** | Saved presets (filters/layouts/sorts), scopable per-role or per-user. |
| **R7** | **Role-based FIELD-LEVEL visibility** | **Built-in (best-in-class)** | Permissions are scoped to collection + action + **explicit field list**, composed via **policies** assignable to roles/users. You grant designers read on manufacturing fields and simply omit pricing fields → they never see them (API and UI). **This is exactly the hard requirement, and Directus does it natively.** |
| R8 | Strong search/filter | **Built-in** | Deep filter operators, full-text/`search`, relational filtering across the API. |
| R9 | S3/R2 attachments + URL fields | **Built-in** | Native S3-compatible storage driver (works with R2); files collection; string/URL fields for external links. |
| R10 | REST/GraphQL + webhooks + SDK | **Built-in (full)** | **Both REST and GraphQL** auto-generated; **official JS/TS SDK**; webhooks + visual **Flows** automation engine. Strongest of the two. |
| R11 | Extensibility WITHOUT forking | **Built-in (real extensions SDK)** | First-class **Extensions SDK**: custom endpoints, hooks, interfaces, displays, layouts, modules, panels, operations — installed as packages, no core fork. The clean-plugin model your principle wants. |
| R12 | Self-host + license | **Buildable — but LICENSE CAVEAT** | Self-hostable (Docker). **MSCL-1.0**: free only if org **< $5M annual income AND < 50 employees** (Open Innovation Grant); otherwise a **paid commercial license** is required. Converts to GPLv3 four years after each release. Internal use is "Permitted Purpose"; you must not remove the license-key mechanism or use it to compete with Directus. |
| R13 | UX maturity for non-technical users | **Buildable — but YOU build the end-user UI** | Data Studio admin app is polished but data-grid-shaped. For designers/sales you'll likely build a tailored front-end (or curate Insights/layouts). The end-user UX is your responsibility — the biggest cost on this side. |

### (2) Can they build 100% on top without forking?
**Yes, technically.** Every requirement — especially the decisive R7 (field-level perms), R1 (true custom entities), R10/R11 (API + extensions) — is achievable through configuration and the official Extensions SDK with **no core fork**. The two non-technical caveats are **(a) the commercial license fee** (almost certainly applies to this company) and **(b) you must build the non-technical-user UI yourself.**

### (3) Biggest blocker
**Not a technical one — it's (a) the LICENSE cost** (a licensing company over $5M revenue / 50 employees must buy a commercial license; budget for it and confirm current pricing), and **(b) UX build effort**: Directus gives you the data/API/permission backbone but the designer/sales-facing app is custom work (React/Vue front-end or heavily tailored Data Studio layouts).

---

## (4) Which is the better base — and why

**Directus, decisively**, for this specific problem.

The company's need is explicitly a **custom-data-model + workflow + field-level-security** problem, and that maps onto Directus's core competencies one-to-one:

- **R7 field-level role-based visibility is a hard requirement and the deciding factor.** Directus does it natively via field-scoped policies; **OpenProject cannot do it without forking core Ruby code.** This alone disqualifies OpenProject under the "no-fork" principle.
- **R1 true custom entities:** Directus collections are real, independent tables (SKU ≠ Design ≠ Style). OpenProject forces everything into work-package subtypes of one entity.
- **R11 clean extensibility:** Directus has a genuine packaged Extensions SDK; OpenProject requires maintaining a custom Docker build (asset recompile) for any plugin — itself a soft fork.
- **R10:** Directus gives REST + GraphQL + official SDK + visual Flows; OpenProject is REST-only with no official SDK.

**The trade-off you're accepting by choosing Directus:**
- You pay a **commercial MSCL license** (org is over the free thresholds).
- You **build the non-technical end-user UI** yourself (R13). OpenProject's mature ready-made PM UI is the one place it clearly wins, but a ready UI that can't enforce field-level security is the wrong tool here.

OpenProject would be the better pick only if (a) field-level security were droppable and (b) you wanted a turnkey PM UI with minimal dev — neither holds for this company.

**Maturity (both healthy):** Directus ~36k stars, frequent releases (v11.x, latest Apr 2026), commercial backing (Monospace). OpenProject ~15k stars, frequent releases (v17.x, June 2026), backed by OpenProject GmbH. Both are actively maintained and production-grade.

---

## (5) Cited URLs

Directus license (decisive):
- https://directus.com/mscl — MSCL license text (4-year GPLv3 conversion; competing-use / license-key restrictions)
- https://directus.com/oig — Open Innovation Grant (free under $5M income / 50 employees)
- https://directus.com/resources/directus-v12-license-change — license-change rationale & thresholds
- https://directus.io/pricing/self-hosted — self-hosted pricing

Directus capabilities:
- https://directus.com/docs/guides/auth/access-control — field-scoped permissions (R7)
- https://directus.io/docs/api/policies / https://directus.io/docs/api/permissions — policies & field-level perms
- https://github.com/directus/directus — stars, releases, SDK, extensions, GraphQL, S3, revisions
- https://docs.directus.io/user-guide/user-management/permissions.html — permission model

OpenProject field-level permission (decisive):
- https://deepwiki.com/opf/openproject/2.4-permissions-and-authorization — resource/module-level only, no attribute-level
- https://community.openproject.org/topics/12502 — "Hide custom field from user group" (confirms limitation)
- https://community.openproject.org/topics/3971 — "Hide work package fields"
- https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/users-permissions/roles-permissions/

OpenProject capabilities:
- https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/manage-work-packages/work-package-workflows/ — statuses/workflows per type+role
- https://www.openproject.org/docs/system-admin-guide/manage-work-packages/work-package-types/ — types & form config
- https://www.openproject.org/docs/api/endpoints/activities/ — journal/activity timestamps (time-in-stage)
- https://www.openproject.org/docs/api/endpoints/work-packages/ — schemas per project+type
- https://www.openproject.org/docs/development/create-openproject-plugin/ + https://community.openproject.org/topics/6422 — plugins = Rails engines requiring custom Docker build
- https://www.openproject.org/blog/openproject-15-1-release/ — hierarchy custom field is an Enterprise add-on
- https://github.com/opf/openproject — stars, releases, GPLv3

**Honest gaps:** I could not pull live exact star counts or current commercial-license dollar pricing for either vendor from a single authoritative page in this pass — confirm Directus commercial pricing directly with their sales/pricing page before budgeting. OpenProject's activity-journal API does record status changes with timestamps, but extracting clean status-transition pairs requires parsing the journal `details` diff (it isn't a structured "status_history" endpoint) — validate the exact payload on your instance before building SLA logic.
